summary: My full name is William Stanley Atherton, born on April 12, 1933, in Ponca City, Oklahoma. I grew up surrounded by entrepreneurial influences, especially from my father and grandfather. Over the years, I've witnessed and been part of many family ventures and developments, and even at 88 years old, I remain active in business deals in Tulsa.

I was born in Ponca City, Oklahoma, and spent my early childhood there. It’s where my father developed Indian Hills Lake and built our family home near the Arkansas River. We lived there until I was about eight years old before moving to Joplin, Missouri.

My mother, Frances Conine, was actually born in Stilwell, Oklahoma, though she often said she was born in Bentonville, Arkansas where her mother was from. Stilwell is part of her roots and family history.

Bentonville, Arkansas, is where my maternal grandmother was from, and my mother always liked to say she was born there, even though she was born in Stilwell, Oklahoma. Bentonville holds a special place in our family story.

My father, Jess William Atherton, was born in 1890 in Caney, Kansas. He was a remarkable man—an engineer and entrepreneur who ran the Atherton Grading Company and other businesses like a laundry service and an airline called Bluebird Airways. He was a pioneer pilot and a hard worker who built Indian Hills Lake and developed real estate around it. I learned a lot about business and perseverance from him.

Caney, Kansas, is where my father, Jess William Atherton, was born and raised, and also where my grandfather lived before participating in the Oklahoma Land Run of 1893. It was the starting point for our family's move into Oklahoma.

My father owned the Atherton Grading Company, which specialized in dirt moving and construction work, especially for the oil industry. He managed teams of horses and mules to build roads and locations for oil rigs, and later cleaned up those sites after drilling was done. It was one of his main enterprises and showed his ability to adapt and find business opportunities.

Drumright was a big oil boom town where my father and his brother worked as roughnecks in their youth. They later started a laundry service there to clean roughnecks' clothes, which was a practical business during that time. Years later, I even saw the old building where that cleaners was located.

Cushing, Oklahoma, was another oil town where my father worked as a roughneck alongside Drumright. It was part of the early oil industry boom that shaped his career and business ventures. Clark Gable also worked around Cushing during that era.

Clark Gable worked around Barnsdall, Oklahoma, and also came to Drumright and Cushing during the oil boom days. My father cleaned his clothes when they were roughnecks, though none of us knew then that Clark Gable would become a major Hollywood star.

Barnsdall was a significant oil discovery area where Clark Gable worked before becoming famous. It was part of the oil boom landscape in Oklahoma that influenced my father's early career and the regional economy.

My father was one of the founders of Bluebird Airways, an airline with three planes including a Jenny and two WACOs. They flew oil men from Pawhuska to Bartlesville or Tulsa, which was quite innovative at the time. He was not only a pilot but also a skilled mechanic, essential for keeping those planes flying.

Pawhuska was one of the towns connected by Bluebird Airways flights, which my father helped operate to transport oil men to Bartlesville and Tulsa. It was part of the oil industry network in Oklahoma during those years.

Bartlesville was another key town served by Bluebird Airways, the airline my father co-founded to fly oil men between Pawhuska and Tulsa. It was central to the oil business and development in Oklahoma at the time.

Indian Hills Lake was a unique development my father created about five miles east of Ponca City on the way to Pawhuska. He built the dam himself with horses and mules, creating a beautiful lake and selling lots for cabins around it. We lived in a house he built near a smaller lake below the main one, and the lake still exists today.

Indian Hills Lake, developed by my father, is located near the Arkansas River, about five miles out of Ponca City on the way to Pawhuska. The river area provided good water and a scenic location for the lake and cabins he developed.

When I was about eight years old, we moved from Ponca City to Joplin, Missouri, due to the economic difficulties during the Great Depression. It was a significant change for our family after my early years in Oklahoma.

My grandfather participated in the Oklahoma Land Run of 1893, the largest land run in U.S. history, which opened the Cherokee Strip for settlement. They came from Caney, Kansas, and settled near Stillwater in Glencoe, Oklahoma. I heard stories about how they raced to claim land by finding survey posts and registering their claims in Perry, Oklahoma.

The Cherokee Strip was the area opened in the 1893 land run where my grandfather and his family claimed land near Stillwater, Oklahoma. It was purchased from the Cherokees and was part of the largest land run in U.S. history. This area became home to our family homestead and businesses.

Stillwater was near where my grandfather settled after the 1893 land run, specifically in a small town called Glencoe nearby. Later, my grandfather moved into Stillwater proper, bought property on Main Street, and started the Atherton Moving Company there.
Glencoe is the small town near Stillwater where my grandfather settled after the 1893 land run. It was part of the family's early homestead before moving into Stillwater itself.

Perry, Oklahoma, was the location of the recording agent's office where land claims from the Cherokee Strip land run were registered. My grandfather had to bring the survey copy there to claim his 160-acre parcel after the run.

My grandfather, James Thomas Atherton, started the Atherton Moving Company in Stillwater after buying property on Main Street. They became known as the only furniture movers who could handle pianos, thanks to their strong men and special dollies. It was a successful business that served the community well.

James Thomas Atherton was my grandfather who participated in the 1893 Oklahoma Land Run and settled near Stillwater. He was an entrepreneur who started the Atherton Moving Company and owned property on Main Street in Stillwater. Sadly, he died when I was very young, but I heard many stories about him from my grandmother.

Henrietta Atherton was my grandmother who lived beyond my grandfather’s death in the mid-1930s. She shared many family stories with me and was a key link to our family history. She passed away sometime after World War II.
The first school I attended was McKinley Grade School in Ponca City, where I completed first and second grades before moving away. It was the start of my formal education in the early 1940s.

As a child, I loved listening to the radio, and one of my favorite programs was Fibber McGee and Molly. It was a main source of entertainment before television, and we eagerly awaited its broadcast. It brought simple joy during those days.

The Great Gildersleeve was another popular radio show I listened to as a kid. Along with Fibber McGee and Molly and Amos 'n' Andy, it was part of the radio entertainment that filled our evenings. Those were good, simple times.

Amos 'n' Andy was one of the radio programs I enjoyed during my childhood. It was part of the lineup of shows that kept us entertained before television became widespread. We looked forward to hearing it on the radio.

The first car I remember my parents having was a Hudson Terraplane coupe. It was a beautiful small car with a single front seat, and I used to sit near the back window. My father also had several pickup trucks for his various businesses.

I vividly remember the Dust Bowl years from 1930 to 1936. The sky would get so dark with dust you couldn’t see the sun, and my mother would wet towels to keep the dust from coming in through the windows and doors. It was hard to breathe outside, and the dust even left a heavy smell inside our home.

The Great Depression hit our family hard, especially in the late 1930s. My father’s oil-related businesses slowed down as companies like Skelly, Sinclair, and E.W. Marland stopped drilling. This economic downturn forced us to move from Ponca City to Joplin, Missouri, seeking better opportunities.

My father worked for Lou Wentz during the oil boom days before the Depression. Wentz was one of the oil men alongside others like E.W. Marland, Skelly, and Sinclair. When the drilling stopped, it affected my father’s grading company and our family’s livelihood.

E.W. Marland was one of the oilmen my father worked for before the Great Depression. Marland’s companies, along with others, ceased drilling during the economic downturn, which hurt my father’s business and contributed to our family’s move to Missouri.

Skelly Oil Company was among the oil companies my father worked for during the boom years. When the Depression hit, Skelly and others stopped drilling, which caused my father’s grading company to lose work and employees, impacting our family’s finances.

Sinclair Oil Corporation was another major oil company my father worked for before the Depression. The halt in drilling by Sinclair and other companies led to a loss of work for my father’s grading business and contributed to our family’s economic struggles.
